title: Chicken parmesan
subtitle: Breaded chicken cutlets baked with tomato sauce and melted mozzarella.
excerpt: The Italian-American adaptation of melanzane alla parmigiana, with pounded chicken cutlets standing in for aubergine. The cutlets are breaded and fried until crisp, then baked under tomato sauce and a blanket of mozzarella until the cheese pulls. Served on pasta or in a sandwich.
sourceNotes: Chicken parmesan is an Italian-American dish that emerged in the eastern United States in the mid-twentieth century, adapting the Campanian melanzane alla parmigiana (aubergines layered with tomato sauce and parmesan) to the post-war abundance of cheap boneless chicken. The dish appears in 1950s American Italian cookbooks; by the 1970s it was a fixture on Italian-American restaurant menus and the basis for the chicken-parm sandwich on a long roll. It is not eaten in Italy as such; the closest Italian dish is the cotoletta alla milanese, served plain with lemon.

content.text: Four chicken breasts, beaten thin, breaded, fried, then baked under sauce and cheese for ten minutes. The trick is not in the assembly but in the order. Fry the cutlets dry and crisp first. Add the sauce only to the top, in a band; leave the edges dry so they keep their crunch. Melt the cheese fast in a hot oven, not for long. The result is a cutlet that has a crisp underside and a melted top, not a cutlet drowned in sauce.

Use a simple tomato sauce: a tin of tomatoes, garlic, oregano, olive oil, twenty minutes on the hob. The cutlet does the work; the sauce just dresses it. Buy mozzarella in a block (the firm cooking kind), not the wet fresh sort. Wet mozzarella weeps water into the dish and softens the breadcrumb.

items.name: Parmesan
items.prepNote: finely grated, mixed into the breadcrumbs

content.text: Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a small saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{garlic}} of chopped garlic and cook for thirty seconds, until fragrant but not coloured. Tip in the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of crushed tomatoes, the {{oregano-dried}} of oregano, a pinch of salt, and a turn of pepper. Bring to a simmer and cook for twenty minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has thickened and the colour has deepened. Taste and adjust.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Lay each {{chicken-breast}} chicken breast flat between two sheets of cling film and pound with a meat mallet (or the back of a heavy pan) until 1 cm thick. Season both sides with salt and pepper. Set up three wide bowls in a line: the {{plain-flour}} of flour, the {{eggs}} beaten eggs, and the {{panko}} of panko mixed with the {{parmesan}} of grated parmesan. Dip each cutlet through the flour, then the egg, then the panko, pressing the crumbs on. Set aside on a plate.

Pour the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil into a large frying pan over medium-high heat. When the oil shimmers, lay in two cutlets at a time. Fry for two and a half minutes a side, until the crumb is deep golden brown. Lift onto a rack or kitchen paper. Repeat with the rest. Do not crowd the pan; the temperature drops and the breadcrumb softens instead of crisping.

Sit the fried cutlets in a roasting pan in a single layer. Spoon a band of tomato sauce across the centre of each one, two tablespoons or so, leaving the edges dry. Scatter the {{mozzarella}} of grated mozzarella across the sauced strips. Bake at 200°C for ten minutes, or until the mozzarella is melted and just beginning to brown at the edges. Pull from the oven, scatter with the {{basil}} of torn basil leaves, and rest five minutes before serving.

items.fix: Press the crumbs into the cutlets after dipping, and let them sit for a couple of minutes before frying. Do not flip the cutlet until the underside has set golden.
items.cause: Coating layers were not pressed on, or the cutlets were moved too soon.
items.symptom: Breadcrumb fell off in the pan

items.fix: Aim for 1 cm thickness, not thinner. Two and a half minutes a side is enough for a 1 cm cutlet; thinner cutlets need only two minutes.
Use block mozzarella for cooking, not wet fresh mozzarella. Sauce only the centre of the cutlet; leave a border of dry crumb.
items.cause: Wet mozzarella used, or sauce covered the whole cutlet.
items.symptom: Cheese went oily and the breadcrumb soaked

items.fix: Cook the sauce uncovered until it holds its shape on a spoon. A thick band should sit on top of the cutlet without running.
items.cause: Tinned tomatoes were thin, or the sauce was not reduced enough.

items.fix: Fry for the full time on each side; the breadcrumb gives you the colour cue. The oven step is for melting cheese, not cooking the chicken; the chicken must be done out of the pan.
items.cause: Cutlets fried too rare, or oven not hot enough to reheat through.
items.symptom: Cutlets are cool in the centre when served

content.text: Aubergine parmesan (parmigiana di melanzane) uses the same technique with sliced and salted aubergine; layer rather than serve as cutlets. For a chicken-parm sandwich (a New York-Italian fixture), slide a cutlet, sauce, and cheese into a long bread roll, run under a hot grill for two minutes, and serve. Veal cutlets pounded thin take the place of chicken in a more traditional version.

Chicken parmesan is the household Italian-American comfort dish. It sits between the home and the diner: ambitious enough to feel like a meal, simple enough that everyone has a version. Served traditionally on a bed of spaghetti dressed with the same sauce, or in a sandwich on a long bread roll. The American version has settled into a stable form over seventy years; the Italian ancestor (melanzane alla parmigiana) is still made daily in Campanian kitchens.
